questions.question: Which of the following best describes how Temporal Cloud handles your sensitive workflow data, according to its security design?

choices.text: Temporal Cloud stores and reads your workflow data to help optimize performance
Temporal Cloud orchestrates encrypted workflows without ever accessing your sensitive data
Temporal Cloud decrypts your data temporarily to ensure workflows run correctly
Temporal Cloud shares workflow data with compliance auditors when required

questions.explanation: Temporal Cloud is designed with a 'provable security by design' principle. This means it can orchestrate and coordinate your workflows without ever needing to read or access your actual sensitive data. Your data stays encrypted end-to-end, so even Temporal itself cannot see what is inside your workflows. This is an important trust feature for organizations handling sensitive information.

questions.question: If your organization wants full control over where and how the Temporal platform is deployed and secured, which option would best fit your needs?

choices.text: Temporal Cloud, because it handles all security automatically
The security whitepaper, because it contains deployment instructions
Self-Hosted Temporal, because it lets you deploy and secure your own infrastructure
Company Security, because it covers all deployment scenarios

questions.explanation: Temporal offers two main deployment paths: Temporal Cloud (a managed SaaS service) and Self-Hosted Temporal (where you run the platform on your own infrastructure). If your organization needs full control over the environment, network, and security configuration, self-hosting is the right choice. It comes with production-ready best practices to help you set it up securely.

questions.question: Temporal Cloud's security features include mTLS. In simple terms, what does mTLS help with?

choices.text: It compresses workflow data to save storage space
It schedules workflows to run at specific times
It ensures that both sides of a connection verify each other's identity before communicating
It automatically retries failed workflow steps

questions.explanation: mTLS stands for mutual Transport Layer Security. Unlike regular TLS (which only verifies the server's identity, like when you visit a website), mTLS requires BOTH the client and the server to prove who they are before any data is exchanged. This mutual authentication makes communication much more secure, ensuring that only trusted parties can connect to your Temporal services. Temporal Cloud uses this as one of its key security features.

questions.question: Which transport security mechanism does Temporal Cloud use to secure communication between clients and the Temporal service?

choices.text: TLS with server-side certificates only (one-way TLS)
Mutual TLS (mTLS), requiring both client and server to authenticate each other
API keys transmitted over HTTPS with OAuth 2.0 token validation
SSH tunneling with public key authentication

questions.explanation: Temporal Cloud uses mutual TLS (mTLS), which means both the client and the server must present and validate certificates. This two-way authentication is stronger than standard one-way TLS because it ensures that only authorized clients can connect to your Temporal namespace. In practice, you configure your Worker and client connections with client certificates issued for your namespace. This is a key distinction from simple HTTPS, where only the server proves its identity.

questions.question: According to Temporal's security model, what is the key principle behind Temporal Cloud providing 'provable security by design' for workflow data?

choices.text: Temporal Cloud stores all workflow payloads in an encrypted database that only the customer can decrypt with their own keys
Temporal Cloud orchestrates encrypted workflows without ever accessing your sensitive data, meaning payload data remains opaque to Temporal
Temporal Cloud runs customer workloads in isolated VMs so the orchestration engine never shares compute resources with other tenants
Temporal Cloud requires all data to be hashed before transmission so the original values are never stored

questions.explanation: Temporal Cloud's security whitepaper describes a model where Temporal orchestrates workflow execution without accessing the actual payload data. Sensitive data in workflow inputs, outputs, and activity parameters can be encrypted client-side using a Data Converter with a custom codec, so Temporal's servers only ever see ciphertext. This is a powerful pattern in practice: your encryption keys never leave your infrastructure, meaning even Temporal as a vendor cannot read your business data. This is different from at-rest database encryption, which Temporal controls.

questions.question: When deploying Temporal in a self-hosted environment for production, which area of security configuration is specifically highlighted as requiring attention beyond what Temporal Cloud handles automatically?

choices.text: Configuring workflow versioning policies to prevent replay attacks
Setting up Activity heartbeat encryption to protect long-running task state
Deploying and securing the underlying Temporal Platform infrastructure itself, including network, persistence, and server configuration
Implementing custom Search Attribute encryption for visibility queries

questions.explanation: With Temporal Cloud, Temporal Technologies manages the infrastructure security for you. When self-hosting, you become responsible for securing the entire stack: the Temporal server processes, the persistence layer (Cassandra or PostgreSQL), network boundaries, TLS configuration between internal components, and more. The self-hosted security guide covers production-ready best practices for this. This is a common consideration when organizations choose between Temporal Cloud and self-hosting — the operational security burden shifts entirely to your team in a self-hosted deployment.

questions.question: A team wants to ensure their workflow payload data — including PII passed as activity inputs — is never readable by Temporal's servers, whether using Temporal Cloud or a self-hosted deployment. What is the correct Temporal pattern to achieve this?

choices.text: Use Temporal's built-in namespace encryption setting to enable server-side AES encryption for all payloads in that namespace
Store sensitive data only in Activity heartbeat details, which are excluded from Temporal's event history storage
Implement a custom Data Converter with an encryption codec that encrypts payloads client-side before they are sent to the Temporal server
Pass sensitive data through Workflow signals instead of activity inputs, as signal payloads are stored separately and can be encrypted at the namespace level

questions.explanation: The correct pattern is to implement a custom Data Converter with an encryption codec. This approach encrypts payload data on the client side (in your Worker and starter code) before it is ever transmitted to the Temporal server. The Temporal server stores and replays the encrypted bytes without being able to read them. Your encryption/decryption keys stay entirely within your infrastructure. This works for workflow inputs/outputs, activity inputs/outputs, signal payloads, and more — anywhere Temporal serializes data. This is the foundation of Temporal's 'zero knowledge' security model described in their Cloud Security Whitepaper.

questions.question: Your team is deploying Temporal self-hosted and the security architect raises a concern: 'Temporal Cloud's whitepaper says it provides provable security by design by orchestrating encrypted workflows without ever accessing your sensitive data. Does this mean workflow input/output payloads are encrypted at rest inside the Temporal server by default?' A junior engineer argues yes, because the whitepaper says Temporal never accesses your data. A senior engineer disagrees. Who is correct, and what is the actual mechanism behind this claim?

choices.text: The junior engineer is correct. Temporal Cloud automatically encrypts all payload data at rest using AES-256, and the server-side encryption is what the whitepaper refers to when it says Temporal never accesses your data.

The senior engineer is correct. Temporal's server (Cloud or self-hosted) stores payloads as opaque blobs by default. The 'never accessing your data' claim refers to the fact that business logic payloads can be encrypted client-side via a custom DataConverter before they ever leave the Worker, meaning the Temporal server only ever sees ciphertext it cannot interpret — this is not a server-side feature but a client-side architecture pattern.

Both are partially correct. Temporal Cloud encrypts payloads in transit using mTLS, and this transport encryption is what the whitepaper refers to. At rest, payloads are stored in plaintext unless you configure server-side encryption in your Temporal Cloud namespace settings.

The senior engineer is correct, but for the wrong reason. Temporal achieves this by storing all payload data outside the cluster in an external blob store (S3 or GCS), and the cluster only stores references. The DataConverter is just a convenience wrapper around this external storage pattern.

questions.explanation:
This question exposes a critical architectural misunderstanding that has real security consequences in production. Temporal's server — whether Temporal Cloud or self-hosted — stores workflow inputs, outputs, activity arguments, signal payloads, and search attributes as blobs in its persistence layer (Cassandra, PostgreSQL, etc.). By default, these blobs are NOT encrypted at rest by the server itself in any special way beyond whatever your database provides.

The 'provable security by design' claim in the whitepaper refers to a fundamentally different mechanism: the custom DataConverter pattern. In Temporal's SDK architecture, every payload passes through a codec pipeline on the Worker before being sent to the server. If you implement a custom PayloadCodec (Go) or PayloadConverter (Python/Java), you can encrypt payloads using your own keys (e.g., envelope encryption with KMS) BEFORE the data leaves the Worker process. The Temporal server then receives, stores, and returns opaque ciphertext. It physically cannot read your business data — not because of server-side access controls, but because it never receives plaintext.

This is 'provable' in the cryptographic sense: even a fully compromised Temporal Cloud operator cannot read your workflow data without your encryption keys. mTLS (option C) encrypts data in transit, which is a separate and complementary concern. The remote data encoder / Codec Server pattern (sometimes confused with option D) is a UI/CLI convenience that lets Temporal Web decode payloads for debugging without exposing keys to the server — the server itself still only holds ciphertext.

The production insight: if you are handling PII, PHI, or financial data in workflows, you MUST implement a custom DataConverter with proper key management. Do not assume Temporal Cloud's infrastructure encryption is sufficient for compliance regimes like HIPAA or PCI-DSS. The DataConverter must be deployed consistently on every Worker and on your Codec Server for the Temporal UI — a gap in either place creates a data exposure risk. This also means your encryption keys become a critical operational dependency: lose the keys, lose the ability to replay or inspect historical workflow executions.

questions.question: An engineering team is migrating from self-hosted Temporal to Temporal Cloud. Their current self-hosted deployment uses a custom authorization plugin that intercepts gRPC calls at the Frontend Service to enforce per-namespace RBAC based on JWT claims. They assume they can port this plugin directly to Temporal Cloud. They also plan to continue using their existing mTLS certificates that workers use to authenticate to the self-hosted cluster. Which of the following most accurately describes the security model differences they will encounter?

choices.text: The migration is straightforward. Temporal Cloud supports custom authorization plugins at the Frontend Service layer, and existing mTLS certificates can be imported into Cloud namespace settings via the Temporal Cloud UI, requiring only a format conversion from PEM to PKCS12.

Temporal Cloud does not support custom server-side authorization plugins — the authorization model is managed by Temporal and uses its own RBAC system via Cloud roles and API keys. However, mTLS IS supported in Temporal Cloud at the namespace level, but the certificates must be issued by a CA that the team controls and registers with their Cloud namespace, not ported from their self-hosted cluster's internal CA.

Temporal Cloud uses API keys exclusively for all authentication and has fully deprecated mTLS support. The team must migrate all Workers to use API key-based authentication, and their existing JWT-based RBAC logic must be reimplemented as a Temporal Cloud Action Policy.

Custom authorization plugins can be used in Temporal Cloud by deploying them as a sidecar to each Worker process, which intercepts gRPC traffic before it reaches the Temporal Cloud Frontend. The mTLS certificates are compatible as long as they use elliptic curve cryptography.

questions.explanation:
This question surfaces a fundamental architectural difference between self-hosted Temporal and Temporal Cloud that teams frequently underestimate during migrations, leading to security model gaps at go-live.

In self-hosted Temporal, the server is yours: you can inject custom Authorizer and ClaimMapper implementations that plug into the Frontend Service's gRPC interceptor chain. This allows arbitrary authorization logic — JWT validation, OPA policy evaluation, external IAM calls — at the server level. This extensibility point simply does not exist in Temporal Cloud, because Temporal Cloud is a managed service where you do not control the server binary. Temporal Cloud has its own RBAC model built around Cloud roles (Admin, Developer, Read-Only) scoped to namespaces, managed through the Temporal Cloud UI or tcld CLI.

However, option C is wrong in a critical way: Temporal Cloud absolutely supports mTLS as an authentication mechanism for Workers and clients connecting to namespaces. The mTLS model in Temporal Cloud requires the customer to manage their own CA and present the CA certificate to Temporal Cloud when configuring the namespace. Workers then authenticate using client certificates signed by that CA. This is actually a stronger security model than many teams realize — Temporal Cloud itself cannot impersonate your Workers because it never holds your CA private key.

The nuance about certificate portability is the trap: the internal CA from a self-hosted cluster (often auto-generated by deployment tooling like Helm charts) is not something you 'register' with Temporal Cloud. You need a proper CA whose trust anchor you control and can register. This often means issuing new certificates even if the cryptographic format is identical.

The production insight: when planning a self-hosted to Cloud migration, audit your security model in three separate layers — (1) transport security / authentication (mTLS or API keys, both supported but configured differently), (2) authorization (self-hosted custom plugins become Cloud RBAC roles; any fine-grained per-workflow authorization must move to the application layer or a Codec Server), and (3) data security (DataConverter encryption is portable and should be your primary confidentiality control regardless of hosting model). Teams that conflate these layers often discover on launch day that their compliance posture has a gap.
